1102.title: Security audit log was cleared
1102.meaning: The Windows Security Event Log was cleared. This often reduces visibility and is a common anti-forensics step.
why_it_happens: Attacker cleared logs to hide activity
investigation: Pivot on the clearing account (Subject user) and host around the same timestamp
Check for privilege escalation events (e.g., 4672) shortly before
Look for suspicious process creation near the event (e.g., 4688)
false_positives: Planned maintenance or log rotation scripts

4624.title: Successful logon
4624.meaning: A logon session was successfully created. Details such as logon type and authentication package explain the context.
why_it_happens: Remote access (RDP/SMB/WinRM) depending on logon type
investigation: Check logon type and source IP/workstation name in the raw fields
Correlate with 4688 process creation after the logon
If it is a privileged user, look for 4672 (special privileges)
false_positives: High volume from normal services and scheduled tasks

4625.title: Failed logon
4625.meaning: An attempt to log on failed. Repeated failures from one source may indicate brute-force or password spraying.
why_it_happens: Service account password out of date
investigation: Pivot on username + source IP/workstation
Look for spikes across many usernames from one source (spray)
Check whether a successful logon (4624) follows the failures

4634.title: Logoff
4634.meaning: A logon session ended. Useful for session tracking and bounding investigation windows.
investigation: Use it to bound activity between 4624 and 4634
Correlate with process/network activity in that time window

4648.title: Explicit credentials used
4648.meaning: A process attempted to log on using explicit credentials (often 'Run as' or programmatic credential use).
investigation: Identify the calling process and target account in raw fields
Pivot on destination host/service and the account used
Look for 4688 process creation and network connections around the time

4672.title: Special privileges assigned
4672.meaning: A logon was granted special privileges. Often indicates an administrative token.
investigation: Pivot on that user + host for follow-on activity (4688)
Check for security setting changes (4719) or group changes (4728/4732)

4688.title: Process creation
4688.meaning: A new process was created. Command line and parent process provide the most investigative value.
investigation: Inspect command line, parent process, and user
Look for LOLBins (powershell, cmd, wmic, rundll32, mshta, regsvr32, certutil)
Correlate with network connections and file writes near the time

4697.title: Service installed
4697.meaning: A new service was installed. Services are a common persistence mechanism.
investigation: Check service name/binary path in raw fields
Pivot on the installing account
Look for 7045 from Service Control Manager as corroboration

4698.title: Scheduled task created
4698.meaning: A scheduled task was created. Common persistence method.
investigation: Inspect task name and command/action in raw fields
Pivot on the creator account and timestamp
Look for follow-on 4688 or file writes

4719.title: Audit policy changed
4719.meaning: Audit policy was modified. This can reduce logging coverage (defense evasion).
investigation: Pivot on the changing account and system
Look for nearby privilege events (4672) and process creation (4688)

4720.title: User account created
4720.meaning: A new local/domain user account was created.
investigation: Identify creator account and new account
Check for group membership additions (4728/4732)
Look for interactive logons by the new account (4624)

4726.title: User account deleted
4726.meaning: A user account was deleted. Can indicate cleanup after compromise.
investigation: Identify actor account and deleted account
Pivot for privilege/group changes before deletion
Review logons and process activity in the prior window

4728.title: Member added to security-enabled global group
4728.meaning: A user was added to a privileged or security-relevant group (potential privilege escalation).
investigation: Confirm which group and which member was added
Pivot on the actor account and timeframe
Check for subsequent privileged logons (4672)

4732.title: Member added to security-enabled local group
4732.meaning: A user was added to a local security group. Often used to grant local admin rights.
investigation: Confirm group/member/actor
Look for 4672/4624 logons using the new privileges

4740.title: Account locked out
4740.meaning: An account was locked due to too many failed logons. Can indicate brute-force or misconfiguration.
investigation: Pivot on the account and caller computer/source
Review preceding 4625 failures
Check for distributed sources across many accounts

4771.title: Kerberos pre-authentication failed
4771.meaning: Kerberos pre-auth failed. Often seen during password spraying against domain accounts.
investigation: Pivot on user and source address
Look for many users targeted from one source
Check if 4624 follows for the same user

4776.title: NTLM authentication failed
4776.meaning: NTLM authentication failed. Repeated failures may indicate spray/brute-force or old credentials.
investigation: Pivot on user and source
Identify whether failures cluster to one host/service
Check for subsequent success (4624)

4104.title: PowerShell Script Block logging
4104.meaning: A PowerShell script block was logged. High value for detecting obfuscation, download-exec patterns, and suspicious cmdlets.
investigation: Review the script block text in Raw JSON
Search for download/execute keywords (iex, downloadstring, base64)
Pivot to 4688 process creation around the same time

7045.title: A service was installed (SCM)
7045.meaning: Service Control Manager reports a new service was installed. Often pairs with Security event 4697.
investigation: Inspect service name and binary path
Pivot on installing account and time window
